Investing
Your Savings Are Silently Drowning
The Simple Math Trick That Proves It
Author: Bart Dority
Originally published: 2025-08-19
Last modified: 2026-03-19
The rule of The Rule of 72 is an easy short hand math equation that you can use to determine how many years it will take to double your money on an investment. Here’s how it works Number of Years = 72 / % of increase It doesn’t matter how much the initial investment is – this equation just tells you how many years it will take before it doubles. So for instance. Say you invest some money into a CD or money-market account, or even some type of bond. If the yield, or compound annual growth rate is 10%, then you will double your investment in 7 years. Pretty cool. Where does this magical number of 72 come from? The 72 is derived from taking the standard compound annual growth rate formula, and solving for 2x. Using the natural logarithmic approximation, we arrive at 69.3 to be more precise. But 72 makes for simpler math and because it has many divisors (2,3,4,6,8,9,and 12) – so it makes mental math a lot easier. Unfortunately, the opposite is also true. Meaning, that if the money you have saved in a savings account isn’t gaining any yield, then it’s not just sitting there waiting for you– it’s actually losing value due to inflation. The rate of inflation varies a lot around the world and from one year to the next. But it’s definitely not the 2 or 3% that the Federal Reserve claims that it is. The real inflation rate in the United States over the last few years has been closer to 7 to 10%. This means that whatever money you have sitting around in a bank account will be worth only half as much 7 - 10 years from now. So for example, say you are diligently ‘saving’ your money from a 9-to-5 job, because you are doing your best to ‘play it safe’… and you manage to save up a little nest egg of 10 or 20k, because you are hoping to put a down payment on a house, because you’ve been told that is the best way to gain wealth. So after 3 or 4 years you have saved up that amount, but in the meantime the real-estate market has gone up another 30%, so now you don’t have enough for a down payment, so you work another 3 or 4 years. In this time, the purchasing power of the 30 or 40k that you’ve diligently been socking away, has lost half of it’s purchasing power. So guess what: It’s still not enough for a down payment on a house. Or consider another example. Say you are incredibly fortunate to have 100 grand, or 300 grand from selling your house… because you were lucky and smart enough to get into the market before 2010, and you put that money in the bank for ‘safe keeping’... In seven years its value will be cut in half. And again another 7 years after that. In the legacy financial system your money is constantly evaporating.
